title: Soy Sauce on Polyester
timeEstimate: 15–35 minutes; cold flush plus salt-rinse, polyester locks brown under heat
stainChemistry: Soy sauce is dark brown caramel color plus salt plus the tannin-like browning compounds from fermentation. Salt is the catch — it acts as a fixing agent that drives the brown deeper into the fiber the longer it sits. On polyester, two more complications stack: heat sets the brown into polyester permanently AND polyester is industrially dyed with heat. The two hard lines: NO hot water and NO alkaline cleaners (baking soda, ammonia, OxiClean alone before peroxide) — alkaline darkens tannin permanently into a deeper brown. The recurring trap is reaching for hot water 'to dissolve' the soy sauce — exactly the wrong move.
whyThisWorks: Cold water flush rinses out the salt before it can fix the color. Mild acid (white vinegar) keeps the tannin on the lighter side of its color range and disrupts the grip on polyester. 3% peroxide on white polyester oxidizes residual brown. Cool wash plus AIR-DRY locks nothing. The honest cap: hot-dried soy sauce on polyester is often a permanent brown shadow because polyester locks color under heat.
scienceNote: Mechanism (pro-facing): soy sauce is melanoidins from fermentation Maillard browning + salt + amino acids + phenolic tannin. Salt acts as a mordant, fixing chromophore to polyester. Pro uses cold acid-side tannin spotter (Chemspec Tannin Spotter or PRENETT-A) plus 3% H₂O₂ for residual chromophore plus acetic acid neutralizing rinse.
defaultAssumption: Sushi night / takeout splash on a polyester shirt or blend, fresh and not yet hot-dried. Hot-dried = often permanent. Performance / specialty / dry-clean-only polyester = pro.

spottingProtocol.technique: Rinse salt before it sets color
spottingProtocol.equipment: Cold running water, white catch towel
spottingProtocol.instruction: Hold the BACK of the stained area under cold running water with a white towel underneath. 60–90 seconds. Cold only — heat sets the brown into polyester permanently. Salt rinses out fast at the start; if you wait, salt fixes the color deeper.

spottingProtocol.technique: Mild acid keeps tannin on light side
spottingProtocol.equipment: 1 tsp white vinegar + 4 tbsp cool water on cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Dab diluted vinegar on the spot, 3–5 min. Disrupts tannin grip and keeps it on the lighter side of its color range. Hidden test on dyed polyester first.
WHITE polyester only. Hidden test on dyed polyester. 3% peroxide damp cloth, 5–10 min, blot. Oxidizes residual brown. NEVER mix with chlorine bleach, ammonia, or alkaline cleaners.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Washing machine, cool water, drying rack
spottingProtocol.instruction: Cool machine wash with normal detergent. AIR-DRY only. Inspect dry — wet polyester sometimes hides residual brown that reappears once dry. NEVER hot dryer until cleared — locks brown into polyester.

steps: 1. Cold water back-flush 60–90 sec — rinse salt before it fixes color.
2. Diluted white vinegar dab, 3–5 min — keeps tannin on light side.
3. 3% H₂O₂ for white polyester, 5–10 min — hidden test on dyed.
4. Cool wash + AIR-DRY. No heat until cleared.
professionalProtocol.temperature: cool throughout — heat sets brown into polyester permanently
warnings: NEVER hot water — sets brown into polyester permanently
NEVER alkaline cleaners (baking soda, ammonia, OxiClean alone) — locks tannin permanently darker
Speed matters — salt fixes color the longer you wait
Hidden test on dyed polyester before vinegar and peroxide
Never mix peroxide with bleach / ammonia / alkaline

homeSolutions.agent: Speed Beats Chemistry — Salt Is the Catch
homeSolutions.instruction: Soy sauce has a lot of salt, and salt fixes the brown color into polyester the longer it sits. So the protocol leads with a fast cold-water rinse, not chemistry. Two hard rules: NO hot water and NO alkaline cleaners (baking soda, ammonia, OxiClean alone before peroxide) — alkaline locks tannin into permanent darker brown.

homeSolutions.agent: Cold Water Flush From the Back — 60 to 90 Seconds
homeSolutions.instruction: Hold the BACK of the stained area under cold running water with a white towel underneath the front. Let the water push the soy sauce out the way it came in. 60 to 90 seconds. Cold only. The salt rinses out fast at the beginning of the rinse — if you wait or use warm water, salt fixes the color deeper into the polyester.

Mix 1 teaspoon white vinegar with 4 tablespoons cool water. Dab on the spot, let sit 3 to 5 minutes, blot. Vinegar keeps the tannin on the lighter side of its color range. Hidden-area test on dyed polyester first — vinegar can lighten some dyes.
WHITE polyester only: dab 3% drugstore hydrogen peroxide on a damp cloth, press 5 to 10 minutes, blot. Oxidizes residual brown. Hidden-area test on dyed polyester first — skip if color transfers.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix peroxide with chlorine bleach, ammonia, or alkaline cleaners (baking soda, OxiClean) — toxic fumes plus locks tannin.

homeSolutions.instruction: Cool machine wash with normal detergent. AIR-DRY only. Inspect dry — wet polyester can hide residual brown that reappears once dry. NEVER hot dryer until the spot is fully cleared. Heat at this step locks the brown into polyester permanently and yellows the fabric over time.

steps: 1. NO hot water. NO alkaline cleaners (baking soda, ammonia, OxiClean alone before peroxide).
2. Cold water back-flush 60–90 sec — speed beats chemistry.
3. Diluted white vinegar dab, 3–5 min — hidden test on dyed polyester.
4. 3% peroxide for white polyester, 5–10 min — hidden test on dyed.
5. Cool wash + AIR-DRY. No dryer until cleared.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if hot water or hot dryer was already used (often permanent on polyester). Stop if alkaline cleaner (baking soda, ammonia, OxiClean alone) was already applied (tannin locked). Stop on performance / specialty / dry-clean-only polyester (pro). Stop after 2 home rounds without progress.
warnings: NEVER hot water — sets brown into polyester permanently
NEVER alkaline cleaners — locks tannin permanently darker
Speed matters — salt fixes color over time
Hidden test on dyed polyester before vinegar and peroxide
Never mix peroxide with bleach / ammonia / alkaline

neverDo: Never hot water on soy sauce + polyester — locks brown forever
Never alkaline cleaners on tannin — permanent darker lock
Never iron the stained area before clearing
Never mix peroxide with bleach / ammonia / alkaline
fiberSensitivities: Dyed polyester: hidden test mandatory before vinegar and peroxide
Performance / specialty polyester (compression, technical, Gore-Tex): pro — coatings react unpredictably
Polyester locks color under heat — strict cool-only discipline

materialWarnings: Salt fixes color — speed beats chemistry on soy sauce
No hot water / no hot dryer — polyester locks brown forever
No alkaline cleaners — locks tannin permanently darker
Performance / specialty polyester = pro from minute one
Hidden test on dyed polyester before any chemistry

escalation.when: Hot water or hot dryer already used (often permanent on polyester). Alkaline cleaner already applied (tannin locked permanently darker). Performance / specialty / dry-clean-only polyester. 2 home rounds plateau. Hidden-area test fails on dyed polyester.
escalation.whatToTell: "Soy sauce on [white / dyed / athletic / specialty] polyester, age [fresh / hours / hot-dried suspected], home-treated with [cold back-flush + diluted vinegar + 3% peroxide on white + cool wash + air-dry]. No hot, no alkaline. [Residual brown / hot-dried suspected / alkaline used]."
escalation.specialistType: professional dry cleaner with polyester experience plus tannin-spotter chemistry

contextQuestions: Fresh, hours old, or hot-dried already?
Has alkaline cleaner (baking soda, ammonia, OxiClean alone) been used?
Light or dark soy sauce (dark soy is more concentrated)?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Salt-fixing means speed beats chemistry — every minute that passes makes the stain harder. Dark / aged soy sauce concentrate is harder than light soy. Polyester adds the heat-lock complication on top of soy sauce's salt-fixing — the same color that polyester is industrially dyed with at high heat is what hot water at home will lock into the fiber permanently. Performance polyester (athletic, technical) goes to pro because the fabric coatings react unpredictably to peroxide.
